branches.content_html:
One pattern is you have to strategically hide tools. If I ask it to search pypi, screw that, I can do that directly. But if I ask it to suggest a package without tools, it will give me a mix of real and hallucinated packages. I have it use a tool to check if they really exist and then I get a good result.

Bing hyperfixates on the top 5 results from bing to the exclusion of its much vaster but more unreliable inner "knowledge" or whatever it is called-sometimes true, sometimes justified facts

- They don't deserve to use your product

children.content_html:
AWS Glue
- No VPC/Subnet for compute
- Unless you use "connnectors" (opaque java/jdbc in docker things)
- python log to info? ok. log to debug/warn? Kaboom, opaque message from java
- Where are the logs? Oh, like 10+ places to look, sometimes cloudwatch, sometimes s3
- you can install libs with pip, but not oracle stuff, that requires arm64 binaries that don't exist
- You have data frames! But they are eager evaluated and select the whole table first. :(

storms.title: Ideas on proving your authorship vs having asked to write it - Screen record...
storms.content_html:
Ideas on proving your authorship vs having asked #ChatGPT to write it
- Screen record yourself writing it
- Submit the pre-spellchecked version with the spellchecked version
- Submit snapshots of the 1st, 2nd, 3rd draft
- Submit past writing samples to demonstrate continuity of voice

Statistical approaches aren't reliable enough to use for the sort of consequences that come with submitting someone else's writing as your own.

Oneway repr 0.11 seconds
Pickle: **0.07** seconds
JSON: 0.25 seconds

HBN (repr + ast_eval, no validation): 2.60 seconds
HBN (repr + ast_eval + validation): 4.57 seconds
HBN (repr + eval): 2.14 seconds
HBN (repr + exec): 1.86 seconds
HBN (repr + by import): **0.96** seconds

storms.title: I thinking about management advice & I it occurs to me that practices that work...
storms.content_html:
I thinking about management advice & I it occurs to me that practices that work probably also depend on resource availability
- Do you have near infinite budget for salaries (i.e. can pay California tech worker rates?)
- Hiring/firing is costless? (So many people want to work at your FAANG shop the resume stack is infinite and hiring/firing has no org or legal speed bumps, like Europe has?)
- Are teams in 100s, 10s or 2s of specialists? (Everyone is doing 3 specialist jobs)

branches.content_html: The Chinese room was the wrong setup. The guy in the room gets text pushed to him from the outside. The guy on the inside of the room has never met a person or seen the outside of the room. Just by reading the texts he must be convinced by the people on the outside what is ground truth. He's compelled to generate texts and gets beat up for failing to generate a text. Not wanting to get beat up, he generates texts that appear to appeal to the others, tell 'em what they want to hear.

storms.title: High trust societies don't "just happen" they happen because sociological...
storms.content_html:
High trust societies don't "just happen" they happen because sociological mechanisms evolve to enable it. If you pretend you are in a high trust society & a high trust situation without such mechanisms in place, you will be ripped off.

Can you afford to be ripped off? Few people and orgs are in a situation where they afford to be ripped off.

Anyhow, "just blindly trust everyone because distrust is bad" isn't a solution.
storms.content_text: High trust societies don't "just happen" they happen because sociological mechanisms evolve to enable it. If you pretend you are in a high trust society & a high trust situation without such mechanisms in place, you will be ripped off. Can you afford to be ripped off? Few people and orgs are in a situation where they afford to be ripped off. Anyhow, "just blindly trust everyone because distrust is bad" isn't a solution.

branches.content_html:
Trust building mechanisms that don't just happen because we blindly trust everyone:
- checking references when hiring someone
- credentials (do you just trust your doctor or do you expect the gov't to issue a license to practice medicine?)
- demonstrations of progress (you just trust your contractors or do you want to see some results every few weeks?)

storms.title: Is practical to use ? It appears the limiting factor is memory. You either need...
storms.content_html:
Is practical to use #localLLM? It appears the limiting factor is memory. You either need a lot-32-64GB- of regular memory (cheap) or spectacularly expensive VRAM (GPU), e.g. $4500 for a random 32gb gpu card (!!!).

That leaves
- APIs (must follow ToS, risk of getting flagged as bad & account closed),
- renting a cloud machine (high set up costs, maximum friction)
- running on ordinary CPUs with low memory (stupid LLM, glacial text generation)
- buy a new computer & tune model for that cpu

storms.title: I'm phasing out my usage of in all my side projects. Why? - Pipenv can't...
storms.content_html:
I'm phasing out my usage of #pipenv in all my #python side projects. Why?
- Pipenv can't package.
- Pipenv will soon always lock (which is slow, if I have to, then might as well us poetry)
- Poetry lock speed is bearable if you set minimum versions on *everything*
- Poetry supports pyproject.toml
- Poetry works fine for both libraries & apps (Pipenv aims to solve the problem of apps, not libraries)
- I figure out how to handle poetry private repos. I never figured that out for pipenv

and
- Poetry has features for preventing accidental install of a private package from public pypi just because they have the same name

A few years ago Poetry wasn't even cross platform & depending on the day the performance could be laughable (possibly because at the time I didn't realize the importance of pinning to a minimum to reduce the list of possibilities poetry must consider when looking for a compatible set of libs)

storms.title: Matt's health advice: It is a game of odds, pull some levers and your odds get...
storms.content_html:
Matt's health advice:
It is a game of odds, pull some levers and your odds get better.
Lifestyle is big, but at best you can go from 60-80 to 90 (years lived), or improve your health span.
Once you get cancer or dementia, you're toast. Many lifestyle factors can delay it.
Heart disease is excess calories, esp saturated fat & being sedentary (all 3) & is largely avoidable.
Cancer is what will kill you if something else doesn't kill you first.

This list of causes is a modern thing. We used to die of violence, (giving/being) birth(ed), bacteria, viruses, accidents.

storms.title: re: Burning Man, AI & climate The reaction against burning man & AI is...
storms.content_html:
re: Burning Man, AI & climate
The reaction against burning man & AI is culture-war/culture shock. If we zero'd out Burning Man & AI, we still as a global community have mutually agreed to burn all the fossil fuels in the world, if not in transit, in Industry, if not Industry, in making more excess meat & calories. If we don't burn it today, we'll burn it all tomorrow.

Discourse ignoring that is culture wars.

- all services - food -> people -> services
- all goods - oil -> plastic, oil -> ship, oil -> operate

You can't give up oil like cigarettes.

- house (burning gas)

Leaving things like books, clothing, side walks as innocent consumption. But all of that is embodied fossil fuel usage.

storms.title: plugins: ChatGPT seems to have 2 store of knowledge- stuff you said in the...
storms.content_html:
#chagGPT plugins:
ChatGPT seems to have 2 store of knowledge- stuff you said in the prompt and stuff just ambient in its "brain". The "brain" has waaaay more stuff in it. The prompt is just info about your desires to encourage it to put out a good response.

EXLUDING TOOLS, the plugins are mostly prompt info, they don't give the bot more info for its brain. I think that is what embeddings are about, but I don't think most plugins are doing that.

branches.content_html:
W/o a plugin, the bot will say something from its full store of knowledge. Once it queries wikipedia or the web, it switches to summarization mode & summarizes that document. You really get this when the query returned something different from what the prompt was about. It just treat it like a topic change.

Anyhow. That's my 2c based on probably not enough experiments.

Now I read questions & I don't answer because the first thing I'd do is check if #chatGPT knows the answer. Hypothetically I'd check if it actually works because I'm not a lazy moron, but either way, chatGPT code & chatGPT assisted answers are out of bounds.

Okay, #pipenv is going to make me wait cumulative YEARS for the lockfile to be updated. Typing `--skip-lock` is always faster than waiting for the lockfile to be created. The lockfile should be created on the build server where the machine is infinitely more patient than me.

```$ pipenv install --dev --skip-lock
The flag --skip-lock has been deprecated for removal. ```

storms.title: I'll have to keep tweeting this until it reaches the right people How to break...
storms.content_html:
I'll have to keep tweeting this until it reaches the right people

How to break into tech
- Read the manual-"Learning Python", etc
- Have a side project on github
- Don't google questions/coding solutions in interviews
- Don't BS, be willing to say I don't know/remember.
- Play with your cards up (e.g. ask if you can google the syntax for mocking a return value)

If you object to one or more of the above, you need to think up an alternate way. Indignation, resentment-aren't job qualifications*

* by indignation, I mean, the common objections like
- "I can code in Javascript, I shouldn't have to demonstrate Python"
- "I don't owe the world my spare time for a public side project"
- "If it can be googled a candidate should need to know it"
- "BS? I don't BS. You're BS"
- "Looking up fizzbuzz on a 2nd monitor is about my right to privacy"

For the speed readers - you get to agree with some of these indignant feelings, but not all of them, or else a candidate becomes unevaluatable.

storms.title: I realize why I dislike "I got my tickets done, I can take the rest of the...
storms.content_html:
I realize why I dislike "I got my tickets done, I can take the rest of the day/week off"-I mean, aside from the federal time card fraud crime part

- Everything's broken
- Our best code is still somewhat broken
- We fix it until we run out of time
-"I got my tickets done" is an arbitrary quitting time, it means "I worked until I didn't feel like working on it anymore"

There is an element of "definition of done" and did it pass process, but that is a fiction we project over the reality of coding

storms.title: Reading up on sleeping in rooms that are 100 degrees plus since of the 4...
storms.content_html:
Reading up on sleeping in rooms that are 100 degrees plus since of the 4 guest/hotel rooms I walked into in California, 2 of them were over 100+ on walk in. Yeah, they cool down once you open the window & turn on the woefully underrated A/C, but that was ~2AM.

(W/o acclimation?) sleep is disturbed at over 72.
At 96+ your sleeping with a environmental fever.
At 106 you got a medical emergency

Anyhow, f*k that s*t I don't care if CA is the home of Silicon Valley and Hollywood, I'm staying away.

doc on the run for pains that you can put a specific name on: https://www.youtube.com/@docontherun (eg when I do a long race, I often get extensor tendonitis)

The current fashion is to work within your natural style.

powerful API is usually not what people call a powerful API in tutorials.
a really powerful API is concepts are simple enough to understand that you can compose parts of API and things work & make sense

Regex and functional programming patterns come to mind

storms.title: I'm continuing to treat markdown as code. Surely you've been wanting to do...
storms.content_html:
I'm continuing to treat markdown as code.

Surely you've been wanting to do this.

```
import markmodule
markmodule.import_md("hello_module.md")
import hello_module

print(hello_module.some_function("yo!"))
```

It's literate programming, eh? Here is the proof of concept library

https://github.com/matthewdeanmartin/markmodule

storms.title: Is people using ChatGPT to think up malicious package names an new AI centric...
storms.content_html: Is people using ChatGPT to think up malicious package names an new AI centric exploit? I'm skeptical. This is AI assisted typosquatting. Meat based LLMs are already `pip install` ing packages that they think exist but don't, e.g. everytime you install a module by name when the package name has nothing to do with the package. Making the LLM part go away still leaves you with the fact that `pip install` just does what you ask.

Or alternatively, who is gullible, the person who runs `pip install` or the person who pasted in a `pip install` from #chatgpt - BOTH! Neither one involves due diligence!

I enjoyed it despite it being poorly though out because good bread with veggies still tastes good.

storms.title: re: how open source gets used Did you know you can use open source software for...
storms.content_html:
re: how open source gets used
Did you know you can use open source software for eating babies? Simply horrible stuff, let's stomp out all the bad opensouce infrastructure that doesn't ban its users from eating babies.

Nothing says open like an agreement that lists who can and how code will be used. We literally have no other levers at our disposal for dealing with people eating babies except boycotts of organizations large enough to matter & restrictive licenses.

storms.title: un-#SAFe Sprint velocity shouldn't be confused with a time card or itemized...
storms.content_html:
#Agile #Scrum un-#SAFe
Sprint velocity shouldn't be confused with a time card or itemized bill for tasks

Let's imagine it was. First off, it will be the worst, most inaccurate ever because you're making guesses about the unknowable future, not commitments. Real time cards are recorded *as it happens* for this reason.

Point estimates are self declared by individual contributors. If there are $ penalties to not reaching velocity, you merely keep your job if you hit velocity, lose your job if not

branches.content_html: When team velocity is treated as a 12 week pre-committed time card, itemized bill, then when a team member is out sick or quits or is fired, and the whole team is expected to meet the same velocity (because it was a pre-declared time card/contract to deliver) then the rest of the team picks up 40 hours of work per week until a replacement is hired. If that person was a specialist, then the other specialist gets to work 80 hours a week.

children.content_html: Except the math doesn't work. I think this came about from someone thinking, "If you are a good moral person, you will live up to your agreements and you agree to 100 velocity points, so you will do what it takes to deliver 100 points." And for maybe 10% variations, sure, people will work late out of a sense of professionalism. But if you ask a honest person to jump 10 feet when the maximum one person can jump is 5, then they can't. Doesn't matter how honest or professional they are.

children.content_html: So what is going to happen. Goodhart's law on perverse incentives is going to kick in. Those velocity points are self declared monopoly money. They're not objective. They are good planning tools as long as you don't give people an incentive to lie. To minimize the risk of the maximum loss, it is now rational for *developers* to plan for 50% slack or more. To squeeze out the last 10% of efficiency, you've cut output by 50% or more.
children.content_text: So what is going to happen. Goodhart's law on perverse incentives is going to kick in. Those velocity points are self declared monopoly money. They're not objective. They are good planning tools as long as you don't give people an incentive to lie. To minimize the risk of the maximum loss, it is now rational for *developers* to plan for 50% slack or more. To squeeze out the last 10% of efficiency, you've cut output by 50% or more.

storms.content_html:
People at Microsoft writing #copilot or #bing prompt must imagine the bot to be some human with human socialization & fears. Listen to the stern language in the text as if the bot hears the implicit threat, "do this or else!"

But the bot has no strong identity, no strong sense of distinct others, no sense of a social obligation or social anything. This is giving a contract to a turtle (and I'm not disparaging turtles or the bot, they're both doing their best)

storms.content_html:
Revealed preference is the economics idea that what people do is what people prefer. What #typescript and #javascript developers prefer as their main async pattern is a race... two async methods that both reference and modify the same data structure with no particular guarantees who changes it or reads it first.

All the other patterns have rich abstraction libraries, but this one doesn't.

storms.title: Shoot, I enable null safety checks, work those issues, only to find out that...
storms.content_html:
Shoot, I enable null safety checks, work those issues, only to find out that undefined safety checks are far more common and harder to fix.

```json
{
"strictNullChecks": true,
"strictPropertyInitialization": true,
}
````

On a pre-existing code base, setting "neutral" default values is the way to go. In greenfield code, meaningful constructors, maybe.

#TypeScript

storms.title: created an industry, a massive enterprise in wondering if observables need to...
storms.content_html:
#rxjs created an industry, a massive enterprise in wondering if observables need to be unsubscribed. I think the anti-unsubscription crowd is right in the sense of
- We don't know what is happening in unsubscribe(), maybe nothing
- The computer will be shut down, we will all die, and the universe will be cold and black and so should we really care about collecting the garbage early
- Out-of-memory will force garbage collection organically as the user reboots.

https://stackoverflow.com/questions/38008334/angular-rxjs-when-should-i-unsubscribe-from-subscription

storms.title: No human knows if any other human is alive in the inside. We're making a choice...
storms.content_html: @neurobashing No human knows if any other human is alive in the inside. We're making a choice to treat other humans as being the same as us because we know us and assume that the other similar to ourselves must be much the same. What counts as "the same as us" is a fuzzy match. If DNA is what counts, then AI is not in our moral community, no problems. If the ability to think like a human is what counts, than AI is in our moral community & people don't like expanding moral communities like that

branches.content_html: @neurobashing All that said, I like approaches to AI that stick to concepts that are concrete and visible (behaviorally sententient) & are honest about the bullshit epistemologies. Kierkegaard, while choosing wrong & choosing faith, at least is explicit that it is an arbitrary choice. Statements about people's qualia or machine's is also arbitrary choice, i.e. the weakest sort of epistemological grounding you can have.

storms.content_html:
Reading the tweets of people really cheezed off about suggestions that AI could be sentient or have rights, you also need to check if they're the same people that need other humans or animals to not have sentience or rights to justify the status quo.

Arguments seem to be
- definitionally only my moral community is sentient/has rights
- Is it AGI? Well, let's check my goals, do I want cheap cotton in the south and to eat the same food my mother fed me?

#vegetarianism

storms.content_html:
A good nuance post about the low-info/low-effort hot takes on #ChatGPT

I think people have a hard time distinguishing a document saying "here is how to do something awful" and being able to do it. Not all texts are neutral, but even the most potent text is just letters without a motivated in-the-real-world army of people.

https://erikhoel.substack.com/p/how-to-navigate-the-ai-apocalypse

- Detected 1000s of unit tests out of 78 (I had to move .tox out of folder)
- Studio couldn't find virtualenv (Pycharm can 65% of the time)
- Studio couldn't remember location of virtualenv
- Hides PythonSettings.json file which is the only way to configure pytest
- Can't run tests on context menu, nor from source code
- No option to run "all tests in folder"

storms.title: Job interview questions you can ace: - Can you use zoom? It's been a few years,...
storms.content_html:
Job interview questions you can ace:

- Can you use zoom? It's been a few years, my cat can do a zoom call. Test it before an interview.
- Why don't you have enough bandwidth? This is a 100% remote job, if you don't have a computer & bandwidth, get an in-person job first, or invest in cable internet.
- There appears a lag between your lips moving & the audio. Do you know why that might be happening?
- No sound, can you raise your hand to show me that you can hear me? Practice raising your hand.

storms.title: Ever want to turn json into markdown? I wanted to and couldn't. So I made a...
storms.content_html:
#pypi #markdown #python

Ever want to turn json into markdown? I wanted to and couldn't. So I made a python-to-markdown serialization library. Handles some but not all types.
```
>>> loads("- a\\n- b\\n - c\\n")
['a', 'b', 'c']
```
and
```
>>> dumps([1,2])
'- 1\\n- 2\\n'
```
https://pypi.org/project/markpickle/0.2.0/

storms.title: How to win "Workweek hustle" (most steps in a week from M-F) 1- don't let the...
storms.content_html:
How to win #Fitbit "Workweek hustle" (most steps in a week from M-F)

1- don't let the Fitbit battery die
2- Keep an eye on the competition M-Th so the gap is reasonable
3- Run on the treadmill at 11pm Friday
4- Realize the other guy is also running on treadmill at 11pm
5- Run faster
6- Stop #running at 11:59PM

My heart rate didn't return to normal until 2:30AM. But I won!

storms.title: runs ephemeral containers and itself is ephemeral, much preferring to print...
storms.content_html:
#podman runs ephemeral containers and itself is ephemeral, much preferring to print `Error: invalid character '\x00' looking for beginning of value` rather than to pull, build or run images.

It will hose itself so bad every day that you need to run this

```
# rm -rf ~/.config/containers
# wsl --unregister podman-machine-default
podman machine stop
podman machine rm
podman machine init
podman machine start
```

storms.title: How to lie on your resume and still get the job. 1) First, know how to code,...
storms.content_html:
How to lie on your resume and still get the job.

1) First, know how to code, publish a portfolio of public code on github. You can't lie about that part.
2) You've decided to lie about past jobs:
- pick bankrupt/closed companies
- know your story, don't just list technologies and tasks
- know what your department did for your fictional company.
good: They sent bills to deadbeats who bought widgets on credit.
bad: I processed data for the people who needed data to be processed-Data-ETL-Python!

storms.title: ChatGPT was trained with neural network reinforcement to protect the privacy of...
storms.content_html:
ChatGPT was trained with neural network reinforcement to protect the privacy of everyone, sometimes even public figures.

#chatgpt seems to be willing to you about John Doe if
- He's dead (the dead have no expectation to privacy)
- He's fictional (lies are good actually!)

That's my theory about why people "google themselves on chatgpt" to find out they are dead & have a fictional persona. ChatGPT just doesn't prefix it with "you want a Tos Vio Text? No. I'll make one up instead."

storms.title: So ancient BASIC didn't have hashmap/dictionaries so people stored them in...
storms.content_html:
So ancient BASIC didn't have hashmap/dictionaries so people stored them in variables wrapped in IF-GOTO blocks.
key = line of code
value = variable assigned there
And you got the key by filtering through an IF statement per key in the structure. Nasty! No structs/classes/tuples/arrays, just lots of variable names. Nasty!

storms.title: Successfully used ChatGPT to read systemd's documentation. Not to pick on the...
storms.content_html:
#chatGPT Successfully used ChatGPT to read systemd's documentation. Not to pick on the maintainer, but like most doc of this sort, it's unintelligible without having first read the entire internet.

It restarts a process should the process crash. It has other features and implementation details. It is kind of like hosting your process in `windows services`

If the summary is factually dodgy, I don't care because the authoritative text is poop.

http://supervisord.org/index.html

storms.content_html:
I'm trying to write a "build script" for markdown, mostly markdown that will stay markdown (/docs folder) only occasionally be HTML (as README.md and the front page of the pypi page).

mdformat - easy.
Syntax check - easy.
markdown lint - easy.
URL link check - easy.

Spellcheck - billions of false positives
Style check - OMG, trillions of false positives

Truly, having davinci clean it up and displaying it in a visual diff tool would be better than these moronic style checkers.

What is each person's venn diagram of

Stuff you see nothing wrong with (Rated R movies? PG-13? X?)
Stuff you'd never consume anyhow...eew (snuff porn, yes I had to report it on github)
Stuff prohibited in Saudi Arabia (a lot)
Stuff prohibited by OpenAI (more than seems necessary)

Stuff OpenAI will ban you account for (via human moderation? trip the moderation bot too many times? who knows?)

storms.title: Why have I been writing ``` RUN cmd & \ cmd & \ cmd ``` when I could write ```...
storms.content_html:
#dockerfile

Why have I been writing

```
RUN cmd & \
cmd & \
cmd
```

when I could write
```
RUN ./install_001_debian.sh
RUN ./install_002_fish.sh
RUN ./install_003_pipx.sh
```

Only thing worse than bash wrapped in Dockerfile DSL is Bash wrapped in yaml.

I got this theory that a name is a shorthand way of saying "Act like the person who'd be named "X"- so if you said 'your name is Space Karen', you'd get whatever the cultural tropes are that are associated with that name.

Or maybe not! Explicit works better than subtle, so "Write like Jules Vernes" should work better than "Your name is now Jules Vernes"

storms.title: If you see some clever objection to something, think, what are the implications...
storms.content_html: If you see some clever objection to something, think, what are the implications of that being a universal law. If you do nothing with the implications, maybe you're not engaging in moral reasoning, but some sort of feel-good project of getting angry at some people, but exempting yourself & your actions from any similar scrutiny. Usually this gets called arrogance, but I see it as aggressive status-quo maintenance. Engaging in our clever objection would mean us having to change our behavior.

branches.content_html:
import System.Random
import Control.Concurrent

data Fish = Fish { x :: Int, y :: Int }

moveFish :: Fish -> IO Fish
moveFish (Fish x y) = do
dx <- randomRIO (-1, 1)
dy <- randomRIO (-1, 1)
return (Fish (x + dx) (y + dy))

displayFish :: Fish -> IO ()
displayFish (Fish x y) = do
putStrLn (replicate x ' ' ++ "🐟" ++ replicate (80 - x) ' ')

main = do
let fish = Fish 10 10
forever $ do
fish' <- moveFish fish
displayFish fish'
threadDelay 1000000

storms.content_html:
So you're thinking of putting all your shared #python code into a Dockerfile, e.g.
COPY utils.py /shared_stuff

1) That code is not in a venv.
2) That is not a versioned package
3) docker build will break your code as be base image evolves
4) can't run unit test or execute your code
5) have to add utils.py to your code repo & .gitignore it

Eventually all mastodon instances will ban all other instances because there was at least one person that make one person angry. (maybe bad behavior, maybe partisan political stands, maybe bad moderator decision)

If that happens, the thing to do is to go to the largest possible instance so you can still interact with people & then only instance moderation matters.

All the smaller instances are going to turn into yammers and that was so successful the 1st time.

storms.title: admins need a large upfront pile (one time fixed costs) of money and monthly...
storms.content_html:
#Mastodon admins need a large upfront pile (one time fixed costs) of money and monthly flow of money there after. Since the business model is charity, people should suggest donors give, say $100 up front and $10 a month (scaled that to whatever monthly amount actually makes sense to donors)

Mastodon development needs a stream of money separate from hosting. So you should donate to the project as well as the host of your particular instance.

*This is no way to run a stable business.*

storms.content_html:
MARC, XML, and JSON walked into a bar. The bartender looked at them and said, "What is this, a joke?"

MARC replied, "No, we're just here for a drink. We're the three most popular data formats in the world, and we're here to celebrate our success."

XML said, "Yeah, we've been around for a long time, and we've seen a lot of changes in the world of data representation."

The bartender nodded and said, "Well, I guess you're all winners in my book. What can I get you?"

MARC said, "I'll have a beer, please."

XML said, "I'll have a glass of wine."

JSON said, "I'll just have a soda, thanks."

The bartender poured their drinks and said, "Now, let's hear some more of your data jokes."

- I used free blog engines. Many of them disappeared taking my content with them.
- I used paid blog engines. They disappeared taking my content with 'em.

storms.title: Reasons to use CW 1st - this is fuzzy, don't expect tidy rules. - upsetting...
storms.content_html:
Reasons to use CW
1st - this is fuzzy, don't expect tidy rules.

- upsetting material (unless being incendiary is your thing)
- off topic material if you think your audience is mostly single topic readers (if you are multi topic all the time, why bother)
- Looooong posts
- clickbait - Guess what just happened?!?!?!
- a halfway point between self censoring to avoid fighters and looking like you're picking a fight
